    • Configure private Virtual Interface (VIF) to connect to Amazon S3 - When using AWS Direct Connect connection, a private Virtual Interface is used to access an Amazon VPC using private IP addresses. It is not a feature of S3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/privatelink-interface-endpoints.html#types-of-vpc-endpoints-for-s3
questions.domain: Design and implement AWS networks

questions.question_text:
An organization has a newly installed 1-Gbps AWS Direct Connect connection. The organization has opted to use cross-connect from the Direct Connect location provider to a port on the router in the same facility. To enable the use of the virtual interface, the router must be configured appropriately.
What are the minimum requirements for your router?
options: BGP Session with MD5, 802.1Q VLAN, Route-Map, Prefix List, IPsec encrypted GRE Tunnel
1-Gbps Single Mode Fiber Interface, 802.1Q VLAN, Peer IP Address, BGP Session with MD5
IPsec Parameters, Pre-Shared key, Peer IP Address, BGP Session with MD5
1-Gbps Multi Mode Fiber Interface, 802.1Q VLAN, Peer IP Address, BGP Session with MD5
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • 1-Gbps Single Mode Fiber Interface, 802.1Q VLAN, Peer IP Address, BGP Session with MD5 — To use AWS Direct Connect, your network must meet the following conditions:
1. Your network must use single-mode fiber with a 1000BASE-LX (1310 nm) transceiver for 1 gigabit Ethernet, a 10GBASE-LR (1310 nm) transceiver for 10 gigabit Ethernet, or a 100GBASE-LR4 for 100 gigabit Ethernet. 2. Auto-negotiation for the port must be disabled. Auto-negotiation is supported only if the port speed is 1 Gbps. Port speed and full-duplex mode must be configured manually. 3. 802.1Q VLAN encapsulation must be supported across the entire connection, including intermediate devices. 4. Your device must support Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) and BGP MD5 authentication. 5. (Optional) You can configure Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) on your network. Asynchronous BFD is automatically enabled for AWS Direct Connect virtual interfaces but does not take effect until you configure it on your router. Incorrect Options:
  • 1-Gbps Multi Mode Fiber Interface, 802.1Q VLAN, Peer IP Address, BGP Session with MD5 — Single mode fiber has a smaller core than multimode and is suitable for long haul installations. Multimode fiber has a larger core and is recommended for fiber runs less than 400 m (1300 feet). AWS Direct Connect uses single mode fiber interface.
  • IPsec Parameters, Pre-Shared key, Peer IP Address, BGP Session with MD5 — VPN connections use IPsec to establish encrypted network connectivity between your intranet and an Amazon VPC over the public internet. Direct Connect bypasses the internet; instead, it uses dedicated, private network connections between your network and AWS. AWS Direct Connect does not use IPSec.
  • BGP Session with MD5, 802.1Q VLAN, Route-Map, Prefix List, IPsec encrypted GRE Tunnel — If you're using AWS Direct Connect to access public AWS services, you must specify the public IPv4 prefixes or IPv6 prefixes to advertise over BGP. The given use case does not need it. AWS Direct Connect does not use IPSec.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html
questions.domain: Design and implement for security and compliance

questions.question_text:
An online retail organization runs its e-commerce website on AWS. The Amazon EC2 instances running the application are fronted by an Application Load Balancer (ALB). Amazon Route 53 provides public DNS services. Different URLs (mobile.shopping.com, web.shopping.com, api.shopping.com) will serve the required content to the end-users.
Which combination of services must be used to serve the content correctly to the end-users? (Select two)
options: Use Host conditions in ALB listener to route shopping.com to appropriate target groups
Use Path component of Redirect actions in ALB listener configuration to route shopping.com to appropriate target groups
Use Host conditions in ALB listener to route $$$$.shopping.com to appropriate target groups
Use Host conditions in ALB listener to route *.shopping.com to appropriate target groups
Use Path conditions in ALB listener to route *.shopping.com to appropriate target groups
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use Host conditions in ALB listener to route *.shopping.com to appropriate target groups
  • Use Host conditions in ALB listener to route shopping.com to appropriate target groups
You can use host conditions to define rules that route requests based on the hostname in the host header (also known as host-based routing). This enables you to support multiple subdomains and different top-level domains using a single load balancer. A hostname is not case-sensitive, can be up to 128 characters in length, and can contain any of the following characters: 1. A–Z, a–z, 0–9 2. - . 3. * (matches 0 or more characters) 4. ? (matches exactly 1 character) You must include at least one "." character. You can include only alphabetical characters after the final "." character. Example hostnames: example.com, test.example.com, *.example.com The rule *.example.com matches test.example.com but doesn't match example.com. Hence, we also need to include shopping.com along with *.shopping.com to include all the hosts serving the requests. Incorrect Options:
  • Use Path component of Redirect actions in ALB listener configuration to route shopping.com to appropriate target groups — You can use redirect actions to redirect client requests from one URL to another. The path component has an absolute path, starting with the leading "/". A path is case-sensitive, can be up to 128 characters in length, and consists of alphanumeric characters, wildcards (* and ?), & (using &), and the following special characters: _-.$/~"'@:+. This option is not useful for the given scenario.
  • Use Host conditions in ALB listener to route $$$$.shopping.com to appropriate target groups — As discussed above, - ., * or ? can be used. '$' is not a valid character.
  • Use Path conditions in ALB listener to route *.shopping.com to appropriate target groups — The path component has an absolute path, starting with the leading "/". Example HTTP path patterns /img/*, /img/*/pics, etc. It does not help while dealing with hostnames, as required for the given scenario.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/load-balancer-listeners.html
